# <Replace with subject — noun-phrase; e.g. "Why we use Diátaxis" or "The crickets split">

<!--
  Explanation mode (per ADR 0004):
    - UNDERSTANDING-oriented: reader wants to understand the "why".
    - Prose-heavy narrative; design docs, ADRs, product intent, rationale.
    - Sections like ## Context / ## Why / ## Background / ## Design /
      ## Tradeoffs are natural here.
    - NO numbered steps (those belong in a how-to).
    - NO Quick Reference tables (those belong in a reference).
    - Reads like a story, not instructions.

  N/A appropriate if the page isn't explanation-shaped. Use a different
  template: tutorial.md (learning) / how-to.md (task) / reference.md (lookup).
-->

<Opening paragraph framing the subject + why this page exists. The reader is here to understand; meet them there.>

## Context

<What the reader needs to know before the explanation makes sense. History, related work, prior decisions. Cross-link to ADRs + design docs + reference pages.>

## <Section heading — noun-phrase or "Why X" framing>

<Prose. Make a point; back it up with evidence or examples.>

## <Another section>

<Prose.>

## Tradeoffs

<Honest discussion of what this approach gives up. Optional but recommended — every design has tradeoffs.>

- <Tradeoff 1: "We chose X over Y because Z; the cost is W.">
- <Tradeoff 2.>

## See also

- [<Related ADR>](<link>) — <when this explanation overlaps with a decision record>.
- [<How-to that operationalizes this idea>](<link>) — <when reader wants to act on the understanding>.
- [<Reference page>](<link>) — <lookup for the artifacts this explanation discusses>.
